questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
Listening for Frogs
When the drainage ditch behind Palmer Middle School was widened in the 1980s, most residents thought of it as a practical improvement. The ditch moved storm water away from houses after heavy rain. Over time, however, the straight concrete channel also created problems. Water rushed through it too quickly, carrying trash into the bay and leaving little habitat for insects, birds, or amphibians.
In 2021, the town began a small restoration project. Workers removed sections of concrete, added stones and native grasses, and shaped the banks so that water would slow down after storms. The town did not attempt to rebuild a perfect natural stream. The ditch still had to protect nearby homes from flooding. Instead, engineers designed a “living channel” that could manage runoff while supporting more plant and animal life.
One unexpected sign of change came at dusk. Science teacher Elena Ruiz began taking students to the channel with clipboards and phone recorders. In the first spring, students heard only traffic and flowing water. By the third spring, they recorded three kinds of frog calls. The class uploaded the recordings to a citizen-science database, where researchers compare animal sounds from different regions.
Ruiz reminds students that restoration is a process, not a quick repair. Some plants fail, storms shift the rocks, and trash still collects after weekends. Still, the return of frog calls suggests that the channel is becoming more than a place to move water. It is becoming a small habitat that students can study in their own neighborhood.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The town transformed a drainage ditch into a channel that still manages water but also supports habitat.
choices.B: Palmer Middle School built a new science laboratory beside the bay.
choices.C: Concrete channels are always safer than natural streams.
choices.D: Students should avoid studying outdoor habitats after storms.

questions.correctAnswerText: The town transformed a drainage ditch into a channel that still manages water but also supports habitat.
questions.explanation: The passage focuses on how the channel was redesigned to manage runoff while supporting more plant and animal life.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Listening for Frogs.”
Why did the town avoid rebuilding a completely natural stream?

choices.A: The town wanted to remove all plants from the channel.
choices.B: The channel still needed to protect nearby homes from flooding.
choices.C: Students preferred to study traffic noise instead of animal sounds.
choices.D: Researchers had already finished comparing frog calls.

questions.correctAnswerText: The channel still needed to protect nearby homes from flooding.
questions.explanation: The passage states that the ditch still had to protect homes, so a completely natural stream was not the goal.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Listening for Frogs.”
Which TWO changes were part of the restoration project?

choices.A: Removing sections of concrete
choices.B: Building new houses along the channel
choices.C: Adding stones and native grasses
choices.D: Replacing the channel with a parking lot
choices.E: Closing the channel during every rainstorm

questions.correctAnswerText: Removing sections of concrete | Adding stones and native grasses
questions.explanation: These two changes are directly listed as part of the restoration project.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Listening for Frogs.”
What can be inferred from the return of frog calls?

choices.A: The channel has become a better habitat for some wildlife.
choices.B: The channel no longer carries storm water.
choices.C: Traffic noise has disappeared from the neighborhood.
choices.D: Students are no longer interested in science.

questions.correctAnswerText: The channel has become a better habitat for some wildlife.
questions.explanation: The return of frog calls suggests that the restored channel can support more animal life.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Listening for Frogs.”
In the sentence “Ruiz reminds students that restoration is a process, not a quick repair,” the word restoration most nearly means [D1].

D1: bringing something back to a healthier condition
replacing something with a larger building

questions.correct: D1=bringing something back to a healthier condition
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=bringing something back to a healthier condition
questions.explanation: In context, restoration means improving the damaged channel so that it functions in a healthier way.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Listening for Frogs.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Students recorded three kinds of frog calls by the third spring.
tiles.B: Engineers designed the channel to manage runoff and support life.
tiles.D: The school replaced the ditch with a gym.

targets.T1: Evidence that the project has had ecological benefits
targets.T2: Evidence that the project balances two purposes
targets.T3: Evidence that the project still has problems

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Students recorded three kinds of frog calls by the third spring.; T2=Engineers designed the channel to manage runoff and support life.; T3=Trash still collects after weekends.
questions.explanation: Each tile matches the evidence requested by the target.

questions.prompt:
Read the community document and answer the question.
Mobile Career Help Van Schedule
The county workforce office will send its Mobile Career Help Van to three neighborhoods this month. The van provides free support for residents who are applying for jobs or training programs.
Services include resume review, online job search assistance, help creating an email account, and basic interview practice. Staff can also explain how to request copies of high school equivalency records, but they cannot issue official records from the van.
Appointments are recommended but not required. Residents with appointments will be served first. Walk-in visitors will be helped as time allows. Each visit is limited to 30 minutes so that staff can assist as many people as possible.
Bring a photo ID if you have one. For resume review, bring a printed or digital copy of your current resume. If you do not have a resume, staff can help you begin one. Visitors who need to use the van’s laptops should save documents to a personal email account because files cannot be stored on public devices.
Locations and times:
Tuesday, May 7: Hillcrest Community Center, 10:00 a.m.–2:00 p.m.
Thursday, May 16: East Market Plaza, 1:00 p.m.–5:00 p.m.
Saturday, May 25: Brookside Adult School, 9:00 a.m.–1:00 p.m.
For appointments, call the county workforce office or sign up through the county website.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To advertise paid computer classes at a private school
choices.B: To explain when and how residents can use free mobile career services
choices.C: To announce that official school records will be issued from the van
choices.D: To require all residents to attend interview training

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain when and how residents can use free mobile career services
questions.explanation: The document lists services, times, locations, and instructions for using the Mobile Career Help Van.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Mobile Career Help Van Schedule.”
Why are visits limited to 30 minutes?

choices.A: To make sure staff can help as many people as possible
choices.B: To prevent residents from bringing resumes
choices.C: To allow workers to close the van after one hour
choices.D: To encourage residents to buy their own laptops

questions.correctAnswerText: To make sure staff can help as many people as possible
questions.explanation: The document says each visit is limited to 30 minutes so staff can assist as many people as possible.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Mobile Career Help Van Schedule.”
Which TWO statements are supported by the document?

choices.A: Residents with appointments will be served before walk-in visitors.
choices.B: Staff can help visitors begin a resume.
choices.C: The van will store visitors’ documents for future appointments.
choices.D: Official high school equivalency records will be printed inside the van.
choices.E: The van will visit Brookside Adult School on a Saturday.

questions.correctAnswerText: Residents with appointments will be served before walk-in visitors. | Staff can help visitors begin a resume.
questions.explanation: Both statements are directly supported by the document.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Mobile Career Help Van Schedule.”
What should a visitor do if using one of the van’s laptops?

choices.A: Save documents to a personal email account.
choices.B: Leave documents on the public laptop for staff to find later.
choices.C: Bring only handwritten documents.
choices.D: Avoid creating an email account.

questions.correctAnswerText: Save documents to a personal email account.
questions.explanation: The document explains that files cannot be stored on public devices.

Which statement from the document best explains why appointments are useful?

choices.A: “The county workforce office will send its Mobile Career Help Van to three neighborhoods this month.”
choices.B: “Staff can also explain how to request copies of high school equivalency records.”
choices.C: “Residents with appointments will be served first.”
choices.D: “Bring a photo ID if you have one.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Residents with appointments will be served first.”
questions.explanation: This sentence explains the practical advantage of making an appointment.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Why Repair Cafes Belong in More Towns
Towns should support monthly repair cafes where volunteers help residents fix small household items. A repair cafe is not a full-service repair shop. Instead, it is a community event where people bring items such as lamps, torn jackets, dull garden tools, or small appliances and work with volunteers to diagnose the problem.
The benefits are practical. Many people throw away items because professional repair costs more than replacement. A lamp with a loose wire or a coat with a broken zipper may end up in the trash even though it could be used for years. At a repair cafe in Madison County, volunteers reported that about 60 percent of items brought in during the first six months were fixed or improved enough to keep using.
Repair cafes can also teach useful skills. Residents who learn how to sew on a button, tighten a loose handle, or identify a worn cord may feel more confident maintaining what they own. These events also bring together retirees, students, hobbyists, and neighbors who might not otherwise meet.
Some business owners worry that free repair events could compete with local shops. Towns can address that concern by limiting repairs to simple items and inviting professional repair businesses to share business cards for larger jobs. A well-run repair cafe does not replace skilled trades. It helps residents make better choices before sending usable items to a landfill.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Towns should support repair cafes because they reduce waste and build practical skills.
choices.B: Repair cafes should replace all professional repair businesses.
choices.C: Residents should throw away small appliances instead of repairing them.
choices.D: Towns should ban volunteers from teaching practical skills.

questions.correctAnswerText: Towns should support repair cafes because they reduce waste and build practical skills.
questions.explanation: The author argues that repair cafes help people keep items longer and learn useful skills.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Why Repair Cafes Belong in More Towns.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that repair cafes can reduce waste?

choices.A: Volunteers and neighbors may meet at the events.
choices.B: Some business owners worry about competition.
choices.C: About 60 percent of items brought to a Madison County repair cafe were fixed or improved enough to keep using.
choices.D: Repair cafes are not full-service repair shops.

questions.correctAnswerText: About 60 percent of items brought to a Madison County repair cafe were fixed or improved enough to keep using.
questions.explanation: This statistic directly supports the idea that repair cafes can keep usable items out of the trash.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Why Repair Cafes Belong in More Towns.”
Which TWO benefits of repair cafes does the author describe?

choices.A: They can help people keep usable items out of the trash.
choices.B: They guarantee that every broken item will be repaired.
choices.C: They can teach residents simple maintenance skills.
choices.D: They require all businesses to provide free labor.
choices.E: They eliminate the need for professional trades.

questions.correctAnswerText: They can help people keep usable items out of the trash. | They can teach residents simple maintenance skills.
questions.explanation: The author describes both waste reduction and skill building as benefits.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Why Repair Cafes Belong in More Towns.”
How does the author respond to the concern raised by some business owners?

choices.A: By arguing that business owners should not be allowed to comment
choices.B: By suggesting that repair cafes handle simple items and refer larger jobs to professionals
choices.C: By claiming that professional repair is never worth paying for
choices.D: By recommending that towns close all repair shops on event days

questions.correctAnswerText: By suggesting that repair cafes handle simple items and refer larger jobs to professionals
questions.explanation: The author addresses business concerns by limiting repair cafe work and connecting larger jobs to professionals.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Why Repair Cafes Belong in More Towns.”

D1: The town sanitation department estimated that small household goods made up nearly one-fourth of bulky waste pickups last year.
The repair cafe was held in a building with blue chairs and large windows.
Several volunteers said they enjoyed drinking coffee after the event.

questions.correct: D1=The town sanitation department estimated that small household goods made up nearly one-fourth of bulky waste pickups last year.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=The town sanitation department estimated that small household goods made up nearly one-fourth of bulky waste pickups last year.
questions.explanation: This evidence would strengthen the argument by showing that discarded household goods are a significant local waste issue.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Why Repair Cafes Belong in More Towns.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Many discarded items could still be useful if repaired.
choices.B: Every resident already knows how to fix electrical problems.
choices.C: Local shops will refuse to repair large appliances.
choices.D: Landfills are open only one day each month.

questions.correctAnswerText: Many discarded items could still be useful if repaired.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that some items are thrown away even though they could continue to be used.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Route Map
For six weeks, Darnell drove Route 18 with another driver beside him. He knew where the bus hissed over potholes, where the school crossing guard waved, and where morning passengers pressed the bell just before the clinic stop. Still, on his first day alone, the folded route map in his shirt pocket felt heavier than the steering wheel.
At 7:20, an older man climbed aboard holding a paper cup and a cane. “You new?” he asked.
“First solo route,” Darnell said.
The man nodded as if this explained everything. “Then I’ll tell you now. The stop after Maple looks close to the corner, but don’t pull too far forward. The curb dips.”
Darnell thanked him and drove on. At Maple, he remembered the warning and stopped short of the dip. A woman with a stroller gave him a grateful look as she stepped down.
By the time the bus reached the shopping center, the morning crowd had thinned. Darnell realized he had been waiting for a mistake large enough to prove he was not ready. Instead, the route had offered him small tests: a tight turn, a passenger’s question, a curb he had not noticed.
At the final stop, he unfolded the map. The lines and arrows looked the same as they had at dawn, but they no longer seemed like instructions from someone else. They looked like a record of where he had already been.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Darnell must repair a bus before passengers arrive.
choices.B: Darnell must overcome his nervousness while driving his first route alone.
choices.C: Darnell argues with a passenger about the fare.
choices.D: Darnell tries to change Route 18 to avoid the clinic stop.

questions.correctAnswerText: Darnell must overcome his nervousness while driving his first route alone.
questions.explanation: The excerpt centers on Darnell’s anxiety and growing confidence during his first solo route.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Route Map.”
Why does the route map feel “heavier than the steering wheel” to Darnell?

choices.A: The map is printed on unusually thick paper.
choices.B: The map represents the responsibility he feels on his first solo route.
choices.C: The steering wheel is broken and difficult to turn.
choices.D: Darnell does not understand any of the street names.

questions.correctAnswerText: The map represents the responsibility he feels on his first solo route.
questions.explanation: The figurative description shows that the map feels emotionally heavy because of Darnell’s responsibility.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Route Map.”
What does the older man’s warning about the curb show?

choices.A: Passengers can help Darnell learn details that are not obvious from the map.
choices.B: The older man wants Darnell to cancel the route.
choices.C: The bus should skip the Maple stop.
choices.D: Darnell should ignore passengers’ advice.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passengers can help Darnell learn details that are not obvious from the map.
questions.explanation: The passenger points out a curb detail that helps Darnell complete the route safely.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Route Map.”
Which TWO details show Darnell gaining confidence?

choices.A: He keeps the route map in his shirt pocket.
choices.B: He stops short of the curb dip after the passenger’s warning.
choices.C: He realizes the route has offered him small tests rather than one large failure.
choices.D: He refuses to answer the older man’s question.
choices.E: He changes the route without telling anyone.

questions.correctAnswerText: He stops short of the curb dip after the passenger’s warning. | He realizes the route has offered him small tests rather than one large failure.
questions.explanation: These details show Darnell applying advice and changing his view of the route.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Route Map.”
The phrase “a record of where he had already been” suggests that Darnell

choices.A: now sees the route as an experience he has successfully completed.
choices.B: plans to throw away the map before the next shift.
choices.C: believes the map is no longer accurate.
choices.D: wishes he had never accepted the job.

questions.correctAnswerText: now sees the route as an experience he has successfully completed.
questions.explanation: The map now represents Darnell’s accomplishment rather than only instructions.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Route Map.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: The folded route map feels heavy in Darnell’s pocket.
tiles.B: An older passenger warns Darnell about the curb after Maple.
tiles.C: A woman with a stroller gives Darnell a grateful look.
tiles.D: Darnell leaves the bus at the shopping center.

targets.T2: A piece of useful local knowledge
targets.T3: A result showing Darnell used the advice well

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=The folded route map feels heavy in Darnell’s pocket.; T2=An older passenger warns Darnell about the curb after Maple.; T3=A woman with a stroller gives Darnell a grateful look.
questions.explanation: The tiles match anxiety, useful knowledge, and the positive result of applying advice.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
What Receipts Can Teach
Many shoppers glance at a receipt only long enough to check the total. However, receipts can help people understand spending patterns that are easy to miss in the moment. A single receipt from a grocery store may not reveal much, but a month of receipts can show how often small purchases become a larger expense.
Financial counselors often suggest sorting receipts into categories such as food, transportation, household supplies, and entertainment. The goal is not to make people feel guilty about every purchase. Instead, categories help shoppers compare what they planned to spend with what actually happened. For example, a person may believe that takeout meals are an occasional treat. After reviewing receipts, the person may discover that takeout has become a weekly habit that affects the grocery budget.
Receipts can also reveal errors. A sale price may not scan correctly, or a returned item may not appear as a credit. Keeping receipts for a short period makes it easier to correct these problems.
Digital receipts can be useful, but they create a different challenge. If receipts are scattered across email accounts, store apps, and text messages, reviewing them becomes difficult. A simple folder or weekly routine can help. Whether paper or digital, receipts are most helpful when they are collected consistently and reviewed with a specific purpose.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Receipts are useful only when shoppers pay with cash.
choices.B: Receipts can help shoppers notice spending patterns and identify errors when reviewed consistently.
choices.C: Digital receipts should be avoided because they are never accurate.
choices.D: Grocery stores should stop printing receipts for customers.

questions.correctAnswerText: Receipts can help shoppers notice spending patterns and identify errors when reviewed consistently.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that receipts are useful for tracking spending and correcting mistakes.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “What Receipts Can Teach.”
According to the passage, why should receipts be sorted into categories?

choices.A: To compare planned spending with actual spending
choices.B: To make every purchase feel like a mistake
choices.C: To replace the need for a bank account
choices.D: To prove that entertainment spending is always wasteful

questions.correctAnswerText: To compare planned spending with actual spending
questions.explanation: Sorting receipts into categories helps shoppers compare their intentions with what actually happened.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “What Receipts Can Teach.”
Which detail best supports the idea that receipts can help correct mistakes?

choices.A: Many shoppers glance at receipts briefly.
choices.B: Receipts can be sorted into categories.
choices.C: A sale price may not scan correctly.
choices.D: Takeout meals may become a weekly habit.

questions.correctAnswerText: A sale price may not scan correctly.
questions.explanation: This detail directly shows how reviewing receipts can help identify errors.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “What Receipts Can Teach.”
What problem does the passage identify with digital receipts?

choices.A: They cannot be used to check prices.
choices.B: They may be scattered across several places, making review difficult.
choices.C: They are illegal in most stores.
choices.D: They always cost extra money to receive.

questions.correctAnswerText: They may be scattered across several places, making review difficult.
questions.explanation: The passage identifies scattered digital receipts as a challenge.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “What Receipts Can Teach.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It explains several ways receipts can be useful and gives examples.
choices.B: It tells a story about one shopper returning an item.
choices.C: It argues that paper receipts are better than digital receipts in every case.
choices.D: It lists stores in order from least expensive to most expensive.

questions.correctAnswerText: It explains several ways receipts can be useful and gives examples.
questions.explanation: The passage discusses spending patterns, error checking, and digital organization.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Neighborhood History Walk
(1) The Westbrook Historical Society is creating a self-guided walking tour of the town’s oldest business district. (2) Volunteers are researching former bakeries, barber shops, theaters, and repair stores. (3) Each stop on the tour will include a short description and a photograph from the society’s archive. (4) [D1], visitors will be able to scan a code on each sign to hear a brief audio story. (5) The project [D2] expected to be finished before the fall festival. (6) Organizers hope the tour will encourage residents to notice details they usually pass without seeing.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=In addition; D2=is
questions.explanation: “In addition” adds another feature of the tour, and the singular subject “project” takes the verb “is.”

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Neighborhood History Walk.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Organizers hope the tour will encourage residents to notice details they usually pass without seeing.
choices.B: Organizers are hopeful that the tour will be something that encourages residents to notice details that are usually passed by them without being seen.
choices.C: Organizers hope the tour will encourage residents, who are residents, to notice details.
choices.D: Organizers hope, in a hopeful way, that residents will notice details due to the tour.

questions.correctAnswerText: Organizers hope the tour will encourage residents to notice details they usually pass without seeing.
questions.explanation: This choice is clear and concise without unnecessary repetition.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?

choices.A: The group of volunteers meet every Thursday.
choices.B: The photographs from the archive belongs in protective folders.
choices.C: The audio stories recorded by the students sound clear.
choices.D: The list of tour stops are posted online.

questions.correctAnswerText: The audio stories recorded by the students sound clear.
questions.explanation: The plural subject “stories” correctly takes the plural verb “sound.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the clearest pronoun reference?

choices.A: When Marcus handed the notebook to Devin, he dropped it.
choices.B: Marcus handed the notebook to Devin, and Devin dropped the notebook.
choices.C: He dropped it when Marcus handed the notebook to Devin.
choices.D: When it was handed to Devin by Marcus, he dropped it.

questions.correctAnswerText: Marcus handed the notebook to Devin, and Devin dropped the notebook.
questions.explanation: Repeating the noun makes it clear who dropped what.

questions.prompt:
Choose the best transition for the blank.
The first speaker described the history of the building. [D1], the second speaker explained how the building is used today.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=Next
questions.explanation: “Next” correctly shows the order of the two speakers.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: After the presentation ended the visitors asked questions.
choices.B: After the presentation ended, the visitors asked questions.
choices.C: After, the presentation ended the visitors asked questions.
choices.D: After the presentation, ended the visitors asked questions.

questions.correctAnswerText: After the presentation ended, the visitors asked questions.
questions.explanation: The introductory dependent clause should be followed by a comma.

questions.prompt:
Which revision best combines the sentences?
The museum opened a new exhibit. The exhibit features local photographs.

choices.A: The museum opened a new exhibit featuring local photographs.
choices.B: The museum opened a new exhibit, it features local photographs.
choices.C: Featuring local photographs, the museum opened.
choices.D: The exhibit opened the museum and local photographs.

questions.correctAnswerText: The museum opened a new exhibit featuring local photographs.
questions.explanation: This revision combines the ideas clearly and efficiently.

questions.prompt: Which sentence maintains consistent verb tense?

choices.A: The guide greeted the visitors and explains the first stop yesterday.
choices.B: The guide greets the visitors and explained the first stop yesterday.
choices.C: The guide greeted the visitors and explained the first stop yesterday.
choices.D: The guide had greeted the visitors and explains the first stop yesterday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The guide greeted the visitors and explained the first stop yesterday.
questions.explanation: Both verbs are in past tense and match the time marker “yesterday.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence places the modifier correctly?

choices.A: Written in faded ink, Carla carefully read the postcard.
choices.B: Carla carefully read the postcard written in faded ink.
choices.C: Carla written in faded ink carefully read the postcard.
choices.D: Carefully written in faded ink, the reading was done by Carla.

questions.correctAnswerText: Carla carefully read the postcard written in faded ink.
questions.explanation: The modifier “written in faded ink” is placed next to “postcard,” the thing it describes.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Keep the Farmers Market Downtown
The Saturday farmers market should remain downtown. Its location near the courthouse makes it easy for residents from several neighborhoods to reach by bus, bicycle, or on foot. The market also brings customers to nearby coffee shops, diners, and small stores. Several business owners report that Saturday mornings are their busiest hours of the week because market shoppers often stay downtown after buying produce.
Moving the market to the fairgrounds would provide more parking, but parking should not be the only concern. The fairgrounds are two miles from the nearest bus stop, and sidewalks along the road are incomplete. A market that depends mainly on cars would be less accessible to older residents, students, and people without reliable transportation. Downtown is not perfect, but it is visible, walkable, and connected to other local businesses. Improving signs and adding temporary bike racks would solve many of the current crowding problems without moving the market away from the center of town.
Passage B: Move the Market to the Fairgrounds
The farmers market has outgrown downtown and should move to the county fairgrounds. The current location creates traffic backups, blocks several storefront entrances, and leaves farmers with limited space to unload produce. Customers often circle for parking and then leave without shopping. A market should be convenient for both vendors and buyers, not just attractive in theory.
The fairgrounds offer wide lanes, restrooms, shade structures, and enough space for more farm stands. The larger site could also include cooking demonstrations, children’s activities, and seasonal festivals that are impossible in the cramped downtown area. Supporters of the downtown location say it is easier to reach without a car, but the town could run a Saturday shuttle from the bus station. With planning, the fairgrounds would allow the market to grow while reducing downtown congestion.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A emphasizes accessibility and downtown business benefits, while Passage B emphasizes space and reduced congestion.
choices.B: Passage A argues that farmers should stop selling produce, while Passage B argues that restaurants should close.
choices.C: Passage A supports moving the market to the fairgrounds, while Passage B supports ending the market.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on school lunches, while Passage B focuses on grocery store prices.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A emphasizes accessibility and downtown business benefits, while Passage B emphasizes space and reduced congestion.
questions.explanation: The two passages disagree about whether accessibility downtown or expanded space at the fairgrounds matters more.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the farmers market.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: The fairgrounds have shade structures and restrooms.
choices.B: Downtown is near bus, bicycle, and walking routes from several neighborhoods.
choices.C: Farmers need space to unload produce.
choices.D: Cooking demonstrations could be added at a larger site.

questions.correctAnswerText: Downtown is near bus, bicycle, and walking routes from several neighborhoods.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports Passage A’s accessibility argument.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about the farmers market.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: The current downtown location creates traffic backups and unloading problems.
choices.B: The fairgrounds would provide enough space for additional activities.
choices.C: Downtown businesses never benefit from market shoppers.
choices.D: The market should be closed during summer.
choices.E: Public transportation is unnecessary for community events.

questions.correctAnswerText: The current downtown location creates traffic backups and unloading problems. | The fairgrounds would provide enough space for additional activities.
questions.explanation: Passage B gives reasons and examples to support both claims.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the farmers market.
Which statement from Passage B directly addresses a concern raised in Passage A?

choices.A: “The farmers market has outgrown downtown and should move to the county fairgrounds.”
choices.B: “Customers often circle for parking and then leave without shopping.”
choices.C: “The larger site could also include cooking demonstrations, children’s activities, and seasonal festivals.”
choices.D: “The town could run a Saturday shuttle from the bus station.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The town could run a Saturday shuttle from the bus station.”
questions.explanation: This statement responds to Passage A’s concern that the fairgrounds are less accessible without a car.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the farmers market.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: The farmers market affects more than just the people selling produce.
choices.B: The market has no loyal customers.
choices.C: The fairgrounds are located downtown.
choices.D: Vendors refuse to participate unless parking is free.

questions.correctAnswerText: The farmers market affects more than just the people selling produce.
questions.explanation: Both passages discuss effects on shoppers, vendors, transportation, and nearby businesses.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the farmers market.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: A count of visitors by transportation method and surveys of vendors and nearby businesses
choices.B: A list of every vegetable sold at the market last year
choices.C: A history of the county courthouse construction
choices.D: A recipe booklet written by local chefs

questions.correctAnswerText: A count of visitors by transportation method and surveys of vendors and nearby businesses
questions.explanation: This evidence would help evaluate claims about accessibility, vendor needs, and business effects.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Carrying a box of programs, the wind blew open the theater door.

choices.A: Carrying a box of programs, the theater door blew open in the wind.
choices.B: The wind, carrying a box of programs, blew open the theater door.
choices.C: While Maya carried a box of programs, the wind blew open the theater door.
choices.D: The theater door carried a box of programs as the wind blew.

questions.correctAnswerText: While Maya carried a box of programs, the wind blew open the theater door.
questions.explanation: This revision makes clear that Maya was carrying the box, not the wind.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The committee reviewed the proposal. The committee approved it unanimously.

choices.A: The committee reviewed the proposal and approved it unanimously.
choices.B: The proposal reviewed the committee and approved it unanimously.
choices.C: The committee reviewed, the proposal approved unanimously.
choices.D: Reviewed unanimously, the proposal committee approved it.

questions.correctAnswerText: The committee reviewed the proposal and approved it unanimously.
questions.explanation: This sentence combines the two actions clearly and grammatically.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses commas correctly?

choices.A: The new sign, which includes a map, will be installed near the station.
choices.B: The new sign which includes, a map will be installed near the station.
choices.C: The new sign, which includes a map will be installed, near the station.
choices.D: The new sign which, includes a map, will be installed near the station.

questions.correctAnswerText: The new sign, which includes a map, will be installed near the station.
questions.explanation: The nonessential clause “which includes a map” is correctly set off by commas.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Make Financial Literacy a Graduation Requirement
High schools should require a one-semester financial literacy course before graduation. Young adults are often expected to make serious financial decisions soon after leaving school. They may sign apartment leases, compare job benefits, use credit cards, borrow for training, or manage irregular paychecks. A course that teaches budgeting, interest, taxes, insurance, and consumer rights would give students tools they can use immediately.
This requirement would not need to replace advanced academic courses. Many schools already offer electives that vary widely in usefulness. A practical financial literacy course could be built into the senior-year schedule or offered online with teacher support. Students who already understand the material could complete projects based on real-life scenarios, such as comparing loan offers or building a monthly budget for a first apartment.
The need is clear. Community credit counselors report that many first-time clients do not understand interest charges, minimum payments, or the difference between gross and net pay. Students should not have to learn these lessons only after making costly mistakes. Schools cannot prepare students for every adult responsibility, but they can provide a basic foundation for decisions almost everyone will face.
Passage B:
Do Not Add Another Graduation Requirement
Financial literacy is important, but schools should not make it a separate graduation requirement. Students already face required courses in English, math, science, social studies, and other subjects. Adding another required course may reduce room for career programs, arts classes, advanced placement options, or credit recovery. For students who are already behind, one more requirement could make graduation harder.
There are better ways to teach financial skills. Math classes can use lessons about interest and budgeting. Social studies classes can discuss taxes, labor, and consumer protection. Career programs can teach paychecks, benefits, and workplace forms. These approaches connect financial topics to classes students already take, rather than creating a separate course that schools must staff and schedule.
Supporters of a required course assume that one semester will change student behavior, but knowledge alone does not guarantee good decisions. Families, wages, emergencies, and access to banking also affect financial choices. Schools should teach financial concepts, but they should do so through existing courses and community workshops instead of adding a new graduation requirement.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether high schools should require a financial literacy course before graduation? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
